title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Effective partnership educates our work force By Tim Bradner Economy (Published: December 29, 2002) The end of the year is a time of assessment, of taking stock. I like to think about what's going well in our state. We hear too much about the problems, and not enough when there's success. The University of Alaska, for example, is doing some things that will help meet our work force challenge. There are two problems here, really. One is that our population is aging. Several industries in Alaska, construction and petroleum among them, are concerned about whether enough young people are being trained to fill gaps in the high-skill engineering and craft trades to replace older workers approaching retirement. The second issue is that 70 percent of our high school graduates don't go to colle…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Secret lives of moose glimpsed COLLARED: Biologists electronically tracked five urban animals for months. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 29, 2002) A cow moose collared by Rick Sinnott, Anchorage area biologist for the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, rests in a small park next to Lake Hood. The moose was collared Oct. 24 on the west side of Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) A collared moose strolls at dusk along the edge of a pond near the Point Woronzof parking areas on Nov. 4. Biologists tracked several moose during 2002. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Daily News archive 2002) (Charles Atkins / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Through frolic and mishap, these were very particular moose. O…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | The finish line Sprint racers Lowery and Frost finally take well-deserved rest By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 29, 2002) For more than two decades, Fairbanks sprint mushers Lloyd Lowery and Kathy Frost spent their lives bound to a training schedule. This was, they knew, the road map to success in any sport. Living by the schedule is difficult. Any top amateur athlete must find 10 to 20 hours per week to train, often more. Some of this time can be squeezed into long, weekend workouts, but even then an athlete faces a daily need for an hour or two. And the time problem only grows when you move from training yourself to training a team of canine competitors. Beyond the exercise comes the need for feeding and cleanup, physical and medical therapy, equipment mainte…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 'Trek' fan beams down to L.A. to fulfill his wish STAR: Anchorage boy and his family mingle with 'Enterprise' cast. By Nikki Jefford Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 28, 2002) Wishes do come true. Just ask 13-year-old Ben Johnson, one of the biggest "Star Trek" fans around. He has boldly gone where most men have never gone before. One week Ben was watching "Star Trek Enterprise" on TV; the next he was watching the filming of a scene live from the set in Los Angeles. The trip was made possible by Make-A-Wish Foundation of Alaska. The nonprofit organization's mission statement is to "grant the wishes of children with life-threatening illnesses to enrich the human experience with hope, strength and joy." Michele Miller, development manager for Make-A-Wish Foundation of Alaska, sa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | DEA bolsters its Alaska presence DRUGS: Agency upgrades Anchorage to district office. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 25, 2002) The U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration is beefing up its presence in Alaska to crack down on the increasing flow of illicit drugs into the state and to curb the illegal substances being manufactured here. The DEA upgraded its Anchorage resident office to a district office in June -- the first step in a long-term plan that includes upgrading a Fairbanks post and opening bureaus in Juneau and the Mat-Su Borough. "We were unstaffed for a while, and we're stepping up to the plate on that," said Zoran Yankovich, who was promoted in June to assistant special agent in charge of the new office. "We're showing that we care about places …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska waters to receive 7.2 million fish KINGS: Plan is to stock Eklutna to take pressure off Ship Creek. By Mary Pemberton The Associated Press (Published: December 26, 2002) The state's fish stocking plan calls for releasing 7.2 million fish into Alaska waters next year and more than 35 million fish over the next five years. The 2003 draft sport fish stocking plan was recently released by the Alaska Department of Fish and Game and makes few changes over the 2002 plan. The plan calls for the release of five different kinds of fish: king salmon, silver salmon, arctic char, grayling and rainbow trout. The plan recommends releasing the same number of fish in 2003 as in 2002. Under the plan, 4.3 million king salmon will released, 1.4 million silver salmon, 89,850 arctic char, 43,900 gray…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 'You gave me a wonderful life' REUNITED: She gave up baby, worried for decades, and then the phone rang. By Ann Potempa Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 25, 2002) After three decades of wondering what happened to the little boy she delivered as an Anchorage teenager, Melinda Coulter has finally found him close to where she left him -- in Fairbanks. In 1968, Coulter spent the Christmas holidays in an Anchorage home for unwed mothers. She had just turned 16 and was pregnant with a little boy. Back then, being pregnant and single was shameful, Coulter said. She can still remember feeling afraid the day her first steady boyfriend and his father came to break the news to her father. Coulter delivered her baby on Feb. 11. She struggled to figure out how to keep him; she said her fat…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: December 26, 2002) YAKUTAT Odor diverts Delta flight from Salt Lake City to Anchorage A Delta Airlines flight on its way to Anchorage from Salt Lake City was diverted to Yakutat on Wednesday after the flight crew noticed a strange odor on board, the airline said. Delta Flight 565 touched down at the Yakutat Airport at 1:45 p.m., according to airline spokeswoman Patsy Mulcahy. None of the 68 passengers on board was injured, she said. The flight had been scheduled to land in Anchorage at 2:35 p.m. The cause of the odor is still unknown, Mulcahy said. All of the passengers were booked on Alaska Airlines flight 93, which usually flies directly to Anchorage from Seattle but made an unscheduled stop in Yakutat, and landed in Anchorage about 5:40 p.m. Bob Miller, the…

title: Create your eCard Revenue at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport was down 2.8 percent, breaking a 10-year revenu…
summary: Create your eCard Revenue at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport was down 2.8 percent, breaking a 10-year revenue growth streak, airport officials said Friday. A rise in security costs as a result of the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks hampered the airport's bottom line, officials said. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Program aimed at expelled students SCHOOL: District hopes its effort will prevent youths from becoming dropouts. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 2, 2002) Expulsion packs a sobering reality check for some students caught twice drinking, doing drugs or toting a bottle or a bong at Anchorage schools. For others, expulsion has the opposite effect. "Some students never come back," said Rita Holthouse, an Anchorage School Board member and former East High principal. "It basically meant the end of their high school because, for whatever reason, they and their families just didn't do what they needed to do to get back in." The Anchorage School District regularly expels about 100 students a year for violating drug and alcohol policies. These make up about three-qua…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Poland honors second 'ski' to lead Alaska MURKOWSKI: Ambassador thanks governor, stumps historians. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 17, 2002) Washington -- The Polish ambassador on Monday thanked Frank Murkowski for the support he showed Poland during his years in the Senate and awarded the new Alaska governor the Commanders Cross of Merit. At the same time, the ambassador shed light on what has apparently been a point of pride among Poles: Alaska's first U.S. administrator, the diplomat said, was a Polish-born Army general, a hero of the Battle of Gettysburg named Wlodzimierz Krzyzanowski. "You are the second governor of Alaska, to my knowledge, who has a surname ending with 'ski,' " said Ambassador Przemyslaw Grudzinski before hanging the gold, white and orang…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Shively joins Holland team FAMILIAR: Ex-DNR commissioner replaces Al Parrish at tourism firm. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 17, 2002) John Shively is Holland America Line's new public face in Alaska. The former state natural resources commissioner spent his first day on the job Monday as vice president of government affairs and community relations at Holland America, one of Alaska's biggest tourism companies. He replaced longtime Holland America executive Al Parrish, who left to run Providence Health System of Alaska. Shively, 58, is a longtime player in Alaska politics, a Democrat who has done stints in the private sector as well as state government. Most recently he's been senior partner of Jade North, a natural resource consulting firm. Before that, Shive…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | City's free gift and food program starts Wednesday (Published: December 17, 2002) The Giving From the Heart program takes place Wednesday through Friday at the Alaska Communications Systems Garage, 650 Telephone Ave. Families with children ages newborn to 18, individuals and senior citizens can apply for free toys, Christmas stockings and holiday food baskets. The event is coordinated by the Salvation Army, U.S. Marine Corps' Toys for Tots program, United Way of Anchorage, Catholic Social Services, Alaska Communication Systems and Conoco Phillips. Distribution will be made according to ZIP code as follows: Wednesday: 99501, 99502, 99503, 99517 Thursday: 99505, 99506, 99508, 99509, 99515, 99516, 99518, 99567, 99577 Friday: 99504, 99507 There are two shifts each day, 9 a.m.-1 p.m. and 3-…

summary: John Norman testified against the south extension of the Coastal Trail at the Egan Center on Wednesday night. "You are going to wreck residential neighborhoods by turning them into parking lots on summer eveings," he said. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ski training programs are plentiful FITNESS: There are year-round programs available for beginners to experts, youths to adults. From tiny tots to hot dogs, ski, snowboard programs abound in the Bowl By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 6, 2002) Not only does the Anchorage area boast excellent areas to ski, but there also are a lot of training programs to help skiers improve their skills. (Photo by Bill Roth / Daily News archive 2002) With more than 150 miles of groomed cross-country ski trails in the municipality and four nearby downhill ski areas, Anchorage has great skiing opportunities. Residents also have excellent ski coaches and training programs to help them take advantage of the long winter. (Photo by Bill Roth / Daily News archive 1999) Click on photo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Forest Service trades parcels SOUTHEAST: Swap involves federal land on Douglas Island. The Associated Press (Published: December 24, 2002) Juneau -- The U.S. Forest Service has agreed to swap federal land on Douglas Island for private land in south Hood Bay on Admiralty Island. Alaska Regional Forester Denny Bschor approved the exchange earlier this month, allowing the agency to trade 220 acres of national forest near the Eaglecrest Ski Area in Juneau for about 100 acres of private land on Admiralty owned by heirs of Jimmy George. The decision will give the Angoon family means to develop guest cabins for about 30 people, a restaurant and homes in a secluded setting near Eaglecrest. It also will allow the Forest Service to acquire an inholding within the Admiralty Island National Monume…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | A fine thing: Club's dancers must cover up PALMER: Funny Farm faces fines if it continues to break borough permitting ordinance. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 20, 2002) The Mat-Su Borough has told Mike Coomler and his wife, Shamarie, who owns the Funny Farm bar on the Glenn Highway near Palmer, that, without a permit, nude dancers may not perform at the bar. The Coomlers plan to fight the restrictions. "We've got calls into about four different lawyers," he said. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge The new owners of the Funny Farm bar on the Glenn Highway outside Palmer say they just wanted to liven up weekend nights. And last weekend, shows featuring nude dancing by five women packed in the customers. "Everybody had a go…

summary: James Havens' small downtown studio was nearly filled with Christmas gifts the local artist and his wife collected for the Alaska Women's Resource Center. Havens credited the October birth of his son Sage with the inspiration to collect the toys. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Worker on medical leave can be fired By Lynne Curry Management (Published: December 23, 2002) Q. Can you fire someone who's on sick leave? One of our key employees caught mono and left on medical leave four weeks ago. I wished him well, said we'd hold down the fort and told him I understood when he called in and asked for extra Family and Medical Leave Act time off to take care of his kids who also got sick. However, when another employee temporarily took over this man' s desk, I learned more than I wanted to about what a flake this guy was. He was supposed to deposit checks. He deposited some and stored others in his desk drawer, along with never-filled-out and thus not-mailed-in compliance reports to several of our funding agencies. In trying to figure out how to handle a particularl…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Land-clearing compromise found ASSEMBLY: Agreement revives measure that stirred fierce opposition. By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 23, 2002) A compromise has revived Anchorage Assemblywoman Janice Shamberg's bitterly contested proposal to require a city permit to clear the trees from undeveloped land. Last month the measure was postponed after it inspired a talk-radio firestorm and an angry public meeting. Mayor George Wuerch and a host of local residents lined up in fierce opposition to what they deemed an attack on private property rights. Shamberg said the critics misunderstood her proposal. She said her goal is to stop large lots from being industrially cleared long before building begins. The denuded tracts pollute air and water with dust and muddy runo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Proposed road measure roils environmentalists 'CLOUDED TITLES': Governor says rule will speed development. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 24, 2002) Washington -- The Bush administration is pushing ahead with a rule that it says will help settle road and land disputes in Alaska and other Western states. Environmentalists, though, are ringing the alarm bells, saying the rule is a giveaway to special interests and could result in roads crisscrossing parks and wild lands. "Treasures like Denali National Park can potentially be compromised by bogus road claims that often have nothing to do with legitimate transportation needs and everything to do with undermining some of the country's most cherished protected areas," said Eleanor Huffines, Alaska director of The Wil…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Kodiak man dies in Homer boat fire The Associated Press (Published: December 5, 2002) HOMER(AP) - A Kodiak man died Sunday in the Homer harbor during a fire on a 58-foot steel commercial fishing boat. Mark W. Dennis, 43, died on the Juneau-based Konrad I, the first fatality from a local fire since the 1980s, according to the Homer Volunteer Fire Department. "The cause of the fire, which originated in the bunk area, is under investigation, and reports have been made to the state Fire Marshall's Office and the Coast Guard," Fire Chief Bob Painter told the Homer Tribune. The vessel was tied to the E float in the harbor. Volunteers responded to the fire at 2:58 a.m. "The fire was first detected by a Homer harbor officer and r…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Real estate market holds its own in 2002 By Chris Stephens Real Estate (Published: December 22, 2002) The commercial real estate market started 2002 out with a bang, coming off an all-time record year of 2001. The pace soon slowed when it became obvious that neither opening the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil drilling nor construction of a gas pipeline from the North Slope was going to happen anytime soon. But nonetheless, 2002 overall has been another active year for investment and commercial real estate in Anchorage. Office rates have stopped their rapid rise and leveled off. Class A rates range from $2.25 to $3.25 per square foot, and Class B is from $1.25 to $1.85 per square foot. In addition to higher rates, nearly all Class A buildings, and many of the higher-end Class B b…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Letters to Santa flood North Pole HELP: Community groups pitch in to help St. Nick answer missives. The Associated Press (Published: December 22, 2002) Fairbanks -- Community groups again this year are helping Santa respond to all those letters and cards from children. Just how to respond sometimes is a challenge. For instance, there was the letter from the little girl sent to Santa in Fairbanks that said, "Dear Santa: Please don't bring my sister a thing. She has been terrible all year." More post offices around the country have stepped in to intercept letters addressed to "Santa, North Pole" and found volunteers to answer them, but a large number still make it all the way to Fairbanks. So far, 28,000 letters have been received. One local group responding to a considerable volume of S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sheet of ice bulls into Kotzebue home BREAKUP Family hurries to escape as mini-glacier bears down on house. By James Mason The Arctic Sounder (Published: June 9, 2002) Kotzebue -- Zona Lie has the cleanest front yard in town. You might say it is glacier clean. While everybody else in town was getting ready for spring cleanup by piling up old junk for the city to haul away, a gigantic sheet of ice sat out front of Lie's place on Shore Avenue, leaving the yard scoured of anything but natural dirt. In the backyard remained a collection of smashed vehicles, snowmachines and a four-wheeler, all destroyed by the mini-glacier that rolled onto her front yard last week. The ice sheet came down from Pipes Spit the morning of May 27 as the Lie family was still in bed. "I was sound asleep and ther…

summary: Andrew Hull, 2, takes a look around the Anchorage Montessori School on Northwood Drive while touring with his dad, Dan Hull, during an open house Saturday. Andrew will attend the school when it opens. Built for a little more than $2 million, the school opens Jan. 6. It replaces a smaller school in Airport Heights, described by one parent as a bit cramped, with basement classrooms. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Hero dog's death sparks action BUDDY: Southeast group starts fund in memory of loyal Labrador. (Published: May 26, 2002) Dog owners in Southeast are outraged at the recent death of Buddy, a black Labrador retriever that had gained stardom for an apparent act of loyalty. They are turning their anger into action. The Gastineau Humane Society announced Thursday the creation of its Friends of Buddy Memorial Fund and is asking for money to help other pets. Buddy became one of Alaska's most famous dogs three months ago after word spread nationwide that he had supposedly watched over his owner's body for 12 days after the Knight Island lodge caretaker was killed by a falling tree. Buddy, the story goes, paced back and forth to the Prince William Sound island's shore looking for help. A group …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | One dead, one hurt in shooting SPENARD: Keith Godfrey faces murder charge after gunfire erupts in parking lot. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 20, 2002) One man is dead, another is wounded and a third is charged with first-degree murder after gunfire broke out early Thursday morning in Spenard. Police say Keith Godfrey, 23, shot and killed Patrick Ramones, 23, and shot and wounded Steven Copeland, 24, during an argument over a woman. Godfrey is being held at Anchorage Jail on murder and first-degree assault charges with his bail set at $50,000. Ramones, Copeland and two women were out celebrating a birthday just before the shooting, said police spokesman Ron McGee. Around 3:20 a.m., Godfrey met the group in the parking lot of the Millennium Hotel on Lake S…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: December 19, 2002) ANCHORAGE Troopers to beef up patrols this weekend for safety's sake Alaska State Troopers on Friday plan to join other state and local police agencies in a nationwide pre-holiday highway safety program, they said Wednesday. Operation CARE -- Combined Accident Reduction Effort -- is a national campaign for safety on the roads that begins 12:01 a.m. Friday and concludes 11:59 p.m. Sunday, troopers said. The last weekend before the Christmas holiday is filled with shopping, parties and visits to family and friends. But it's "also the weekend during which driver inattention, excessive speed and driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs usually cause more traffic accidents" than normal, troopers said in a written statement. Troopers, worki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Virus outbreak brings investigators to Ketchikan NORWALK? Officials ask for help in slowing spread of the illness. The Associated Press (Published: December 13, 2002) Ketchikan -- Ketchikan is experiencing an outbreak of a Norwalk or Norwalk-like virus and state health officials are asking for the public's help to slow its spread. "What we really want people in Ketchikan to do is to call the public health center and report if they've been ill with these signs and symptoms," said Dr. Joe McLaughlin of the state Division of Public Health's Section of Epidemiology. "The bottom line is how we can prevent more cases." On Monday, the Epidemiology Section learned of numerous instances of people in Ketchikan experiencing a sudden onset of nausea, vomiting and diarrhea, McLaughlin said. The cas…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | NOAA crew member dies when launch capsizes The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (August 14, 3:25 p.m. ADT) - A crew member aboard a ship doing survey work was killed when the launch he was piloting capsized in Prince William Sound, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration said Wednesday. The crew member was identified as Eric Koss, 30 of Woodinville, Wash. He was piloting the smaller boat Tuesday when it was struck by large waves. Koss was a member of the crew of the NOAA hydrographic survey ship Rainier. Two other crew members, David Fischman and NOAA Corps Ensign Jennifer Johnson, made it safely to shore. "Eric was a skilled seaman and valued contributor to our mission of collecting survey data to update the region's nautical charts," said Capt. James Gardner, commanding officer…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | The great speckled bird A rare great spotted woodpecker brings fame to Caswell Lake By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 17, 2002) The downy woodpecker is found throughout Southcentral and Southeast Alaska. At 7 inches, it is similar to the hairy woodpecker, which is 9 inches tall and also inhabits the same range. The downy's bill is also smaller than the hairy's. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Myrtle Heinrich scans the trees near her home with Grete Perkins of Talkeetna. Heinrich says she has hosted about 200 people at her home who have come to see the great spotted woodpecker. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Todd and Pam McGrath of Los Angeles showed up at Myrtle Heinrich's house earlier this month to view the rare woodpecker, which …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Pair recognized for bringing islanders together LEADERS: Men work to debunk myths about Samoa, Tonga cultures. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 3, 2002) Sailitai Maga and Talakai Finau are proving that Samoans and Tongans have more in common than a taste for island breezes and stuffed taro leaves. They were recently recognized with the Racial Justice Award of the Alaska United Methodist Women. Maga is the lay pastor of the Samoan Fellowship, which meets at Turnagain United Methodist Church. Finau is active in the laity of the Tongan Fellowship at Anchor Park United Methodist Church. Both have worked to correct misunderstandings about the two cultures, said the Rev. Dennis Holway of the Superintending Team, Alaska Missionary Conference (the governing body of Al…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | House eyes state-owned gas line PROJECT: Measure calls for report on future feasibility. The Associated Press (Published: April 23, 2002) Juneau -- A bill calling for possible state ownership of a natural gas pipeline from the North Slope passed the House on Monday. The measure would require the Joint Committee on Natural Gas Pipelines to prepare a report by January 2004 on the feasibility of such a state-owned line. If the report says the project is feasible, the bill would launch the Alaska Gas Corp., a state entity that could issue bonds to finance the project and contract with private companies to build and operate the line. The three major oil companies on the North Slope -- BP, Exxon Mobil and Phillips Petroleum -- have so far indicated their analysis indicates a $15 billion to $…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | North Pole looks at noise, gun law DEBATE: Mayor, councilman at odds over firearms. The Associated Press (Published: August 29, 2002) North Pole -- City council members considering a night noise ordinance scrambled into action recently after discovering there was no local law prohibiting the firing of guns within city limits. The council is considering a noise ordinance aimed at barking dogs and loud music. The measure would require quiet hours in the city between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. on weekdays and 11 p.m. to 7 a.m. on weekends. The gun issue came up after a resident complained to Mayor Jeff Jacobson about his neighbors shooting animals within city limits -- which is not now prohibited by ordinance. "There was nothing on the books," the mayor said. Since guns make loud noises, a provis…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoor Hotline (Published: December 8, 2002) HOLIDAYS Learn where you can cut your own Christmas tree Want to cut your own Christmas tree this year? You can cut down holiday trees within much of Chugach National Forest and on parts of the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge, but no cutting is allowed on national park or state park lands. The guidelines for cutting down trees within the Chugach National Forest are as follows: All areas of the Chugach National Forest on the Kenai Peninsula are open for Christmas tree cutting except Portage Valley and Turnagain Pass. The Portage closure extends from the Seward Highway to Portage Lake. The Turnagain Pass closed area extends from where Bertha Creek crosses the Seward Highway north to the National Forest boundary and for half a mile on each side…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: December 8, 2002) ANCHORAGE Fish Board declines to change novel, controversial Chignik co-op A contentious three-day Board of Fisheries meeting ended Saturday with no significant changes to the novel salmon cooperative begun last summer at Chignik. Under the co-op, a majority of commercial salmon fishermen work together to catch sockeye rather than race one another for fish. The aim is to use fewer boats to harvest the fish, with all co-op members sharing in reduced expenses and higher profits. Fishermen who didn't join complained that the board last year allocated too much of the Chignik salmon run to the co-op fleet. But the board late Friday refused to take away fish from the co-op or adopt other proposed changes. The co-op is seen by many people as an impo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Don't let home profits go through the roof Claire Ramsey Housing (Published: April 28, 2002) There are a number of ways to lose money in real estate. One expensive mistake is thinking you can sell your home with a roof near the end of its life expectancy, thus passing on the replacement cost to the new buyer. Unfortunately, you may not slip past the lender, appraiser or home inspector who will look for at least two years of useful life on the roof. If you think you may need a new roof before you sell your property, here are a couple of tips to minimize your cost, while maximizing your efforts. Assuming you have the most common roof type -- asphalt single -- the first tip is to select materials that fit your needs. There are three basic types of roofing materials. The typical asphalt sh…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Study says more samples needed before Anchorage port expansion The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(December 6, 6:25 p.m. AST) - A new study says extensive sampling of the ocean floor of Cook Inlet is needed before a proposal to expand the Port of Anchorage can go forward. The report, completed in August but not yet made public, says the underwater soils in Knik Arm may not be strong enough to support the planned expansion. The city-funded report says the underwater soils in Knik Arm may not support a proposed open-cell sheet-pile dock design submitted by Anchorage-based Peratrovich, Nottingham & Drage. The report was conducted by David Chapman, geotechnical engineer for Colorado-based LACHEL & Associates. and Gabriel Fernandez, a geotechnical engineer at the University of Illinois, at a cos…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Work-related deaths reverse positive trend 2001: For second year in a row, number of fatalities increases. The Associated Press (Published: December 6, 2002) While the number of work-related deaths in Alaska declined in the 1990s, the number last year increased for the second year in a row, according to the Alaska Department of Health and Social Services. There were 64 work-related deaths in 2001, up from 54 the previous year, and up from a low of 42 in 1999. The increase between 2000 and 2001 amounts to an 18.5 percent increase. Deborah Hull-Jilly, lead field investigator for the agency's Occupational Injury Prevention Program, blamed last year's increase on two incidents that together killed 25 people -- the sinking of the Arctic Rose fishing vessel in the Bering Sea on April 2 and t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Lieutenant governor candidates speak out GOP: Crowded field of hopefuls try to distance themselves from the pack. By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 25, 2002) Lt. governor candidates Robin Taylor, from left, Diane Benson, Ernie Hall and Daniel DeNardo talk before the start of Wednesday's candidate forum at the Palmer Moose Lodge. Loren Lehman, Sarah Palin and Gail Phillips also attended the event sponsored by the Greater Palmer Chamber of Commerce. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge PALMER -- Lieutenant governor candidate Robin Taylor, in a red-white-and-blue American flag jacket, thundered from the podium at the Palmer Moose Lodge. The state needs to work to open up Alaska lands for development and get out of the way, he said.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Business People (Published: December 16, 2002) Greg Wolf was named the new executive director of World Trade Center Alaska. Wolf begins his new post on Jan. 1. Currently, he is director of the state Division of International Trade and Market Development. He was appointed by Gov. Tony Knowles in 1998. Prior to that, he was vice president and director of overseas projects for the Anchorage Economic Development Corp., and has worked in the state's trade office in Tokyo. Alaska's SuperStation hired Casey Woodruff as traffic coordinator and Cindy Boisvert as production assistant, both at ABC Channel 13 in Anchorage. Woodruff has a bachelor's from the National School of Broadcasting in Seattle, Wash. He has been a morning radio personality and program director and operations manager in news …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Judge halts new timber sales TONGASS: Ruling orders Forest Service to stop selling in roadless areas. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 12, 2002) An Anchorage federal judge told the Forest Service on Tuesday to stop authorizing new timber sales in roadless areas of the Tongass National Forest in Southeast Alaska. U.S. District Judge James K. Singleton ruled in a case that pits the Sierra Club against the Forest Service over how the federal agency manages the 17 million-acre temperate rain forest, the country's largest national forest. Singleton's latest order clarifies an injunction he imposed this spring that halted new timber sales on nearly 10 million roadless acres. The injunction stems from a 2001 decision by Singleton. The judge found that the Forest Service v…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cruise ships increase Homer visits TOURISM: The Chamber of Commerce readies for a busy 2003 visitor season. The Associated Press (Published: December 10, 2002) Homer -- Cruise ship travelers will be a more regular sight on the Homer Spit in 2003. The Homer Chamber of Commerce cruise ship committee said nearly 15 bookings have been secured for the port. "Anytime you can have a group of visitors coming into the community, all with the intent of doing something, looking for something to do, that's really exciting," chamber director Derotha Ferraro told the Homer Tribune. The most frequent guest will be the 330-foot, 115-passenger Spirit of Oceanus, operated by Alaska Sightseeing Cruise West, which made its first stop in Homer last summer. Spirit of Oceanus will dock at least a dozen times…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Defendants ask to withdraw plea in murder-for-hire case The Associated Press ANCHORAGE(December 11, 6:05 p.m. AST) - Two Anchorage defendants accused of trying to hire a hit man to kill a federal judge and prosecutor have asked a U.S. district judge to withdraw their guilty pleas. Arnold W. Flowers and Sompong Khamsomphou were indicted in February on charges that they plotted hits on four people connected to an impending bank fraud case against them. As part of an agreement with the government, they pleaded guilty in June to conspiracy to kill a witness, conspiracy to kill an officer or employee of the United States and conspiracy to commit bank fraud. But their new lawyers say their first attorneys gave them bad advice, according to arguments filed in U.S. District Court. According to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crash landing closes Kenai's main runway The Associated Press KENAI(December 4, 4:05 p.m. AST) - The Kenai Municipal Airport's main runway was closed for nearly an hour Tuesday afternoon when a twin-engine Cessna crashed upon landing. Soldotna plane owner Joel Caldwell was returning with a friend from Anchorage when he noticed something wrong just before landing. "The landing gear didn't lock," he said. Neither occupant of the plane was injured. Caldwell said there was only minor damage to the propellers and nose of the plane after the aircraft collapsed on its front landing gear. Caldwell said the lock light in the cockpit that indicates all landing gear is prepared for contact did not come on. As he approached the runway, he cut the engines and decreased his air speed. "We had a tota…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Six saved from gas poisoning 911: Dispatcher recognizes flu-like symptoms caused by carbon monoxide, sends multiple crews. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 13, 2002) Six people, including three children, were rescued early Thursday morning after a 911 dispatcher realized a caller and her family were suffering carbon monoxide poisoning during the call, fire officials said. The three adult victims and the children, ages 5, 7 and 9, were rushed to area hospitals. All six had recovered by Thursday evening and were released, according to officials at Alaska Regional Hospital and Providence Alaska Medical Center. Names of the victims and the dispatcher were not released. Anchorage Fire Department spokesman Tom Kempton said the 911 call came in around 7:55 a.m. A …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Crewman dies, two injured on log carrier south of Adak The Associated Press KODIAK(December 10, 6:30 p.m. AST) - One crewman died and two others were seriously injured on board a log carrier 180 miles south of Adak on Monday. The captain of the 465-foot log carrier Borca contacted the Coast Guard requesting medical assistance. He said the ship encountered rough weather and three crewmen, working below decks, were banged around. The captain said the injured crewmen suffered broken bones and possible head injuries. After consulting with one of its flight surgeons, the Coast Guard instructed the Borca's captain to change course and head toward Adak. The Borca is home-ported in China. A Jayhawk helicopter and crew departed Kodiak at about 3:30 a.m. A Coast Guard C-130 and crew with an addi…

summary: | | New labor head to 'clean out' agency PROMISE: Department has become too partisan, says Murkowski spokesman. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: December 11, 2002) Juneau -- Gov. Frank Murkowski vowed this week that his new labor commissioner would "clean out" a state department that has deteriorated and become highly politicized. Exactly what problems there are with the department is a question for which Ed Flanagan would like an answer. "I don't know what he's talking about or where he's getting that from," said Flanagan, the former labor commissioner under Democrat Gov. Tony Knowles, who earlier this year responded publicly to a series of political ads aired by a Republican-friendly trade group attacking the Knowles administration. Americans for Job Security, a nonprofi…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Language barriers tackled By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 9, 2002) It was a scenario that shouldn't have happened: A Spanish-speaking woman hospitalized by a car crash still didn't know three weeks after the accident that she was paralyzed for life. Monica Perez-Verdia is the one who told her. Without an interpreter, the woman didn't understand her injuries. "Patients go home all the time with information they don't understand," said Perez-Verdia, director for the Diabetes Assistance and Resources program at the American Diabetes Association. She is also president of Clase, a local group that helps organize and represent the Hispanic community. At an Immigrant Rights Coalition meeting Thursday, P…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: December 5, 2002) ANCHORAGE Sixth-grade boy injured after being attacked by dog at Williwaw School A young boy was injured Wednesday afternoon when he was mauled by a dog in an East Anchorage school yard, Anchorage police said. The boy, a sixth-grader, received serious but not life-threatening injuries to his legs and arms and was taken for treatment to Alaska Native Medical Center, authorities said. The incident happened about 3:45 p.m. at Williwaw Elementary School, police said. After the attack, a man called police to say that the same dog was menacing him, said spokesman Ron McGee. Police notified Anchorage Animal Control, which caught the animal and took it into custody, McGee said. The dog was described both as a bulldog and a white pit bull. School was …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community profile (Published: November 24, 2002) NORTHWAY JUNCTION POPULATION: 72 (2000) LOCATION: On the Alaska Highway, 5.5 miles northeast of Northway at the foot of Cheneathda Hill in the Tetlin National Wildlife Refuge. DESCRIPTION: Northway consists of three dispersed settlements: Northway Junction, at Mile 1264 of the Alaska Highway; Northway, on the airport spur road; and the Native village. The area was traditionally Athabaskan, though road construction and the airport have brought a permanent non-Native population, now more than 40 percent of the whole. Most wage employment is through state highway maintenance and services for travelers. A general store, motel, garage and Bureau of Land Management fire-guard station provide limited work. Firefighting and construction jobs bri…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | State totals up damage caused by earthquake CABINET: Commissioners to weigh in on disaster decision. By Tataboline Brant Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 6, 2002) Damage assessment teams descended Tuesday on the Alaska communities hardest hit by Sunday's 7.9-magnitude earthquake, hoping to get a sense of the quake's fury. The sight wasn't pretty, with Tetlin and Mentasta Lake suffering the worst of the lot, said Kerre Fisher, spokeswoman for the Alaska Division of Emergency Services. In Tetlin, roads, houses, a clinic and a bridge were damaged. In Mentasta Lake, houses were knocked off their foundations, fuel tanks were spilled and roads were cracked. Other communities visited included Healy, Slana, Cantwell and Northway, where the airport is deemed unusable. Details about tho…

summary: | | Vehicle crashes kill five in Anchorage DEATHS: Police can't yet explain deadly night on local streets. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 29, 2002) Five people died in four separate vehicle accidents in Anchorage between Wednesday night and Thursday afternoon. Brian D. Litzinger and Walter Graham Jr. died in a single-car crash on the Glenn Highway near the Muldoon Road exit about 12:30 a.m., according to Anchorage police. Two hours later, Stephen N. Indahl died in a single-vehicle wreck on Raspberry Road near Northwood Drive. On Thursday afternoon, Heidi Wallace died after a pickup truck crossed into her lane on the Old Glenn Highway in Birchwood and struck her northbound Subaru head on. And a woman who has not been identified died Wednesday evening when she trie…

By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 24, 2002) Brenda Mahan, Wendy Briggs and Penny Booher made a quilt to hang in the new Anchorage Fire Department Firehouse 1 downtown. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Family members of the crew at the city's oldest fire station have quilted a wallhanging as colorful as the station's 75-year history. Among its blocks are the patch worn by Station 1 crew members and the Anchorage Fire Department's patch. A skull-and-crossbones beneath a fire hat represents the hazardous materials services housed at Station 1. Patches on other blocks are for the station's paramedics and the Intern…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | BP bowing out of ANWR lobbying group HURT: Oil giant's pullout will take $50,000 yearly from Arctic Power's pocketbook. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 26, 2002) Washington -- As the lobbying group Arctic Power gears up for yet another year of trying to persuade Congress to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to drilling, Alaska's second-largest oil producer has dropped out of the effort. BP Alaska told Arctic Power last week that it would no longer contribute money to the group and that the company's representative on the board was resigning. "It's not a message about ANWR at all," said BP Alaska spokesman Paul Laird. "It's simply a business decision that BP made that it no longer wants to be part of the debate." He said BP paid annual dues of $50,000. "It…

He and other executives handed over $1 million, the first installment of a $5 million fine agreed to after the U.S. Coast Guard discovered evidence of waste oil dumping on their freighters. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ruling: Refiners were overcharged OIL: Commission orders refunds, sets lower rates for Tesoro, Williams. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 30, 2002) An order issued this week by the Regulatory Commission of Alaska could lead to lower oil revenue for the state. The commission found that operators of the trans-Alaska pipeline overcharged to move crude oil for refinery operators Tesoro Alaska Co. and Williams Alaska Petroleum Inc. These companies use roughly 10 percent of the oil moved down the pipeline to make petroleum products for local use, with the remainder going aboard tankers to other states. The commission set lower rates and ordered refunds, possibly tens of millions of dollars, for in-state oil moved during 1997-2000. The commission said the rates for thos…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Oil spill worker dies of injuries COOK INLET Man had been hit in the head by a cable last week. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 23, 2002) A Cook Inlet oil spill response worker has died of injuries suffered in an accident aboard a barge last week. Jonathan Bradley Mudrick, 31, of Soldotna, died Saturday at Providence Alaska Medical Center in Anchorage. Mudrick was hit in the head by a steel cable Thursday morning while working aboard a barge in Nikiski Bay, said Doug Lentsch, general manager of Cook Inlet Spill Prevention & Response Inc. The accident occurred as a crew of CISPRI workers were bringing up the barge's two anchors, one on each end, to move it down to Seldovia for the winter, Lentsch said. The barge is loaded with boom, skimmers and other oil spill cl…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Gas contract carries Agrium STOPGAP: Unnamed supplier to keep chemical plant going for 30 days. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 23, 2002) The Agrium chemical plant in Nikiski will have enough gas for the next 30 days to operate up to capacity, Agrium Inc. announced. The 30-day contract with an unnamed Cook Inlet supplier is a stopgap measure to cope with unexpected cuts to gas delivery from Agrium's main supplier, Unocal. Bill Popp, oil and gas liaison for the Kenai Peninsula Borough, said Agrium's decision to keep mum on its interim supplier's name is part of a strategy on both sides to get the best price. Calgary, Alberta-based Agrium bought the plant in 2000 from Unocal, a major oil and gas producer in the Cook Inlet region. Agrium had a deal with the petr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: November 22, 2002) Alaska Airlines adds Miami flight Alaska Airlines on Thursday launched once-daily nonstop service between Seattle and Miami. Last month the Seattle-based airline added New York/Newark to its route system. It also recently added Denver and Boston as it continues its expansion that began with Chicago and Washington, D.C., routes in the past couple of years. Chamber sets new long-range goal The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce has adopted a new long-range plan, the group said. The chamber's new goal is to "be effective as a business leader by supporting and focusing our broad-based membership in their efforts to grow Anchorage into a Premier American City." Among the plan's objectives are creation and execution of a proactive communicatio…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Forecast is for spectacular meteor storm LEONIDS: But clouds could obscure unusual cosmic shower. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 17, 2002) The skies over North America could dazzle with a spectacular Leonid meteor storm on Monday night when Earth passes through dust left by the Comet Tempel-Tuttle. The prospect has excited scientists and skywatchers across the world, especially since it may be the last big blast from the Leonids until the 22nd century. Forecast for Anchorage: clouds. "It looks like more miss than a hit," specialist John Stepetin with the National Weather Service said Friday. "Right now -- and you can take it with a grain of salt -- Monday is looking mostly cloudy." In case the clouds peel back, people have two viewing chances. An early showin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | State OKs plans for new Kenai gas line SPUR: Work to link new Ninilchik reserves could begin in January By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 30, 2002) The state has given its blessing to a new Kenai Peninsula natural gas pipeline linking new reserves in Ninilchik to an existing pipe network that connects clear to Anchorage. Construction on the new $25 million spur could begin as soon as January. It could be finished and operational by the end of October 2003, developers say. The line will be able to handle three times more gas than it will initially carry, and officials hope it will boost development of new fields near Ninilchik. The 12-inch diameter, high-pressure pipe will be buried mostly along the Sterling Highway and Kalifornsky Beach Road rights of way. The pro…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 'We the People' looks to the future RALLY: 'The Next 10,000 years' is theme of annual Native march. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: August 22, 2002) The people who have inhabited Alaska's shores, hunted its tundra and fished its rivers for the last 100 centuries will express their commitment to future generations today with a march and rally in downtown Anchorage. "The Next 10,000 Years" is the theme of the fifth annual "We the People" march, which starts at 2 p.m. at the state courthouse on Fifth Avenue and I Street. From there marchers will wind through downtown and end up at Delaney Park Strip. The theme reflects the Native American view of humankind's place on earth, said march co-chairman Larry Merculieff. "We are only borrowing the land and water from future generati…

(Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Young AFN conference attendees are taking advantage of Internet access terminals provided this year in the lobby of the Egan Center. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kodiak museum seeks to concerve Alutiiq language By MEGAN HOLLAND, The Associated Press KODIAK(November 22, 5:30 p.m. AST) - The Alutiiq Museum is working to revive a critical piece of Alutiiq heritage by developing a dictionary and establishing a center for the disappearing language. Today, the median age of Alutiiq speakers is 67. From a total population of 3,000 Alutiiq people, only about 400 still speak the language. In 1970, there were roughly 1,000 speakers. The museum and Alutiiq community are working to reverse the downward trend. Shauna Hegna, Alutiiq Language Coordinator at the museum, will spend the next year working closely with elders, tribal councils, the Kodiak Area Native Association, Native corporations and other organizations of the Kodiak archipelago to plan a compre…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Elders court works to save troubled village teens YUP'IK: Community offers an alternative to state justice system. By Danielle Wolffe The Tundra Drums (Published: December 26, 2001) Emmonak -- A 16-year-old boy sits on a wooden bench before a court of elders, wiping away tears as he admits to drinking alcohol because he was angry with his parents. The confession is not designed to elicit sympathy from a state magistrate. The boy has agreed to appear before a special court set up by the elders in his community. The Emmonak Elders Group was formed in 1999 when six elders came together to respond to parents' complaints that too many youths were racking up criminal records. Though elders had always been integral to the community, the group was officially recognized by the Emmonak Tribal Co…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Murkowski says he'll support mining with roads, permitting The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (August 24, 4:10 p.m. ADT) - Republican candidate for governor Frank Murkowski laid out an aggressive pro-mining policy Friday, saying the state should help build roads to open remote mining areas. Murkowski listed a handful of potential roads to the Pogo mine near Delta Junction, the Donlon Creek mine in southwest Alaska, the Pebble Copper deposit near Iliamna and the Taku and Stikine road projects in Southeast. "You're what opened this country," Murkowski told the Alaska Miner's Association. "I'll pursue every avenue possible to pursue mining." Murkowski's pitch for the road projects, which have a combined price tag of hundreds of millions of dollars, was part of a string of policies he said he …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Diomede will be fined for illegal whaling QUOTA:Residents were not authorized to hunt gray whales. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 9, 2002) Diomede, the Bering Sea village that lost a resident during a whale hunt in late June, did not have a gray whale quota and will be fined for the infraction by the International Whaling Commission. Maggie Ahmagoak, executive director of the Alaska Eskimo Whaling Commission, is investigating the incident. She said any penalty is likely to be in the range of $100 to $1,000, because the whaling expedition was undertaken out of need. "In this case the infraction would probably be at its lowest level because of their desperate need to feed their people," Ahmagoak said. "We do not permit them" to hunt gray whales, she added, "but they wi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Talk tackles troubled group of orcas in Sound BIOLOGIST: Craig Matkin tracked dwindling AT1 group last summer. By Doug O'Harra Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 20, 2002) The lone killer whale would cruise up to seals hauled out on ice near the Chenega Glacier in western Prince William Sound. "Then he'd swim out of the bay, acting like he's leaving," said biologist Craig Matkin, the region's leading killer whale researcher. In a series of remarkable observations made last summer by scientists and tour boat operators, the big male hunter would return without breaking the surface. "When he comes back, holy cow, is he ever quiet," Matkin said. "He stays down a long time. It's incredibly stealthy." title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | New Apples for teachers COMPUTERS: Some educators get laptops; labs need upgrades. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 7, 2002) Instead of using notebooks and card files to store student assessment scores, teacher Deneen Bozeman can now use her new laptop computer. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge The disparity among computers in Anchorage schools is clear when you wander the classrooms of Campbell Elementary School. In its computer lab, students use old Macintosh models that crash regularly and are unable to access the Internet. On the teachers' desks sit sleek new Apple iBook laptops, among 579 recently bought by the district. The purchase is part of a larger plan that also includes better machines for Anchorage students. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | GAO: Few use medical marijuana REGISTER: Most are 40 or older, use drug for MS, muscle spasms or pain. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 30, 2002) Washington -- Few people have registered to use medical marijuana in Alaska and three other states with a drug-law exemption, and of those who have, more than 70 percent are 40 or older, according to a government study released Friday. The General Accounting Office, responding to a congressman's request, looked at the experiences of Alaska, California, Hawaii and Oregon. They are among at least eight states that have passed laws exempting certain patients from state prohibitions on marijuana use. The laws, mostly the result of voter initiatives, are in conflict with federal drug law, which doesn't allow marijuana use fo…

summary: In Alaska Outdoors News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Business | Sports Even Thanksgiving has its wild side By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 24, 2002) Anchorage hunter Dave Adams prides himself on providing wild game for his family year-round. So what's on his table come Thanksgiving? No, not duck. Not goose or moose either. The Adamses' table will feature what is for them an unusual dish: domestic turkey. "We don't buy store-bought turkey anytime except Thanksgiving and Christmas," Adams said. "Turkey is just good. Whatever you say about it, it's good." Adams may be typical of many area hunters. Most contacted this week said they eat so much wild game over the course of the year that the seasonal turkeys are a welcome change. Others like the id…
